coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot sensitized integrative g-C3N4/AgCl Hybrids: An synergetic interaction for enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic process
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Matheswaran, Priyadharshini
Thangavelu, Pazhanivel
Palanivel, Baskaran
coredata.dc:description: Rational design of organic-inorganic heterojunction photocatalyst has become a research hotspot of recent researchers due to their abundance and cost effectivity. Herein, we present the integrated photocatalyst carbon dot (CD) decorated on g-C3N4/AgCl heterojunction synthesized by simple impregnation method. The primary confirmation for the as-prepared samples has been carried out by various characterization techniques. From the obtained results, the ternary nanocomposite demonstrated a prominent photocatalytic activity which was almost 10 and 51 fold higher than pure g-C3N4 in the degradation of organic dyes. The reasons behind the improved efficiency are (i) The introduced CD facilitates broader absorption in the visible region because of π-conjugated CDs and provide favorable electron kinetics by creating trap states at the CD/g-C3N4 interface. (ii) The AgCl acts as an electron sinker and reduce the carrier’s recombination. To justify the photocatalytic activity, a possible mechanism was proposed and verified by trapping experiments. Finally, this approach could be a new one in an organic-inorganic hybrid photocatalyst for the degradation of organic dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic pollutant
Organic-inorganic hybrid
Heterojunction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) FESEM images of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs, (c) (d) and (e) TEM images of CDs (inset: SAED pattern), Particle size distribution, g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs and (f) HRTEM image of g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs.
XRD patterns (a), FTIR spectra (b), UV-DRS (inset: Tauc plot) and PL spectra (c & d) of the prepared samples.
Typical (a) survey spectrum and high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Ag 3d, (e) Cl 2p, and (f) O 1s elements in g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs.
Dye degradation plot of (a) MB, (b) Rh B and the Pseudo-first order kinetic plot of (c) MB and (d) Rh B dyes.
(a) Scavenger test spectra ofMB, (b) Reusability and (c) PXRD pattern before and after four cyclic runs of g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs.
Schematic illustration of possible photocatalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs ternary nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot sensitized integrative g-C3N4/AgCl Hybrids: An synergetic interaction for enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Smart Materials Interface Laboratory, Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem 636 011, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Smart Materials Interface Laboratory, Department of Physics, Periyar University, Salem 636 011, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational design of organic-inorganic heterojunction photocatalyst has become a research hotspot of recent researchers due to their abundance and cost effectivity. Herein, we present the integrated photocatalyst carbon dot (CD) decorated on g-C3N4/AgCl heterojunction synthesized by simple impregnation method. The primary confirmation for the as-prepared samples has been carried out by various characterization techniques. From the obtained results, the ternary nanocomposite demonstrated a prominent photocatalytic activity which was almost 10 and 51 fold higher than pure g-C3N4 in the degradation of organic dyes. The reasons behind the improved efficiency are (i) The introduced CD facilitates broader absorption in the visible region because of π-conjugated CDs and provide favorable electron kinetics by creating trap states at the CD/g-C3N4 interface. (ii) The AgCl acts as an electron sinker and reduce the carrier’s recombination. To justify the photocatalytic activity, a possible mechanism was proposed and verified by trapping experiments. Finally, this approach could be a new one in an organic-inorganic hybrid photocatalyst for the degradation of organic dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last few years, there has been intense research in the field of semiconductor photocatalysis addressing environmental remediation. Mostly eco-friendly, more substantial solar conversion efficiency, controllable bandgap for redox process and chemically inert nature of synthesized materials were usually considered as the rubric of the photocatalyst. Unfortunately, traditional homogeneous photocatalyst bears innate defects such as activation over UV irradiation, sluggish redox reaction, etc., which actively restrains its practical application. Fabrication of heterogeneous photocatalyst could efficiently widen up the absorption of the solar spectrum thereby promotes charge carrier separation [1–4]. Hence, compared with homogeneous counterpart heterogeneous photocatalyst often provide more appealing features.

Amidst various heterogeneous systems, Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) an organic semiconductor has considered as a promising photocatalyst due to its fascinating physiochemical behavior because of the appropriate band gap, superior chemical, and thermal stability, etc., Despite all, the efficiency of g-C3N4 is restrained by low charge mobility, high recombination rate of photo-induced excitons, and relatively limited surface area. Therefore, various strategies have been adapted to optimize the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4. Out of various strategies, a great deal of consideration has been paid for the synthesis of silver halide based g-C3N4. This was due to the phenomenon that AgCl possesses favorable band alignment of bandgap 3.25 eV (i.e. from conduction (+3.19) to valence band (−0.06 eV)) by which separation of the photo-generated electron-hole pair was increased under visible light irradiation [5–8]. Regretfully, there is still one more serious barrier, i.e., narrow light harvesting over the solar spectrum, remains unsolved holding back the photocatalytic application of g-C3N4/AgCl nanocomposites.

Also, to facilitate the higher separation of photo-generated e−/h+ pair of g-C3N4/AgCl, several strategies have been employed including transition metal doping, modification with organic or inorganic semiconductor, coupling with carbonaceous material as well as noble metal incorporation [9–15]. One of the best strategy among all to enhance the performance coupling of carbon dot, a photosensitizer [16,17]. It could efficiently act as an electron donor as well as an electron acceptor, and this property offers enhanced light harvesting over the broad spectrum of visible light. Besides it also serves as electron mediator between AgCl and g-C3N4 [18–20]. Currently, a good deal of interest has been paid to the facile synthesis of carbon dots from biowaste through simple hydrothermal method which offers self-passivation without further treatments. As a precursor biomass were utilized, it is not merely because of its abundant resource, cheap, and eco-friendly just due to its sustainability. In addition biowaste has a large amount of carbohydrates with O and H components such as glucose, starch, sucrose, cellulose etc., renders functionalised carbon dots. Among a variety of bio resource Citrus X sinensis (orange peels) were the major unexploited resources and widespread biomass rests all over the world where the vast leftovers had not effectively utilised in large scale. Ceaseless attempts have carried out to the transition of several types of carbohydrates present in Citrus X sinensis into 5-hydroxymethylfurfural an inexhaustible biowaste - deduced unit [21]. A. Prasannan et al., has employed orange peel waste to prepare fluorescent C-dots by hydrothermal carbonisation method and the as-prepared C-dot with ZnO composite under UV irradiation exhibits efficient decolourisation of naphthol blue-black azo dye [22]. Similarly Siti Fatimah et al has synthesized C-dots from orange waste through microwave assisted technique and investigated its consequence of matrix composition on the optical attributes [23]. Hence, in this study, we attempt to construct novel heterojunction g-C3N4 /AgCl/CD photocatalyst via facile impregnation method by which we aim to maximize the potential of g-C3N4. The as prepared samples were analysed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Consequently, the g-C3N4/AgCl/CD nanocomposite shows a significant enhancement in decolouration of two cationic dyes such as methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (Rh B) compared with g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/AgCl composites due to absorption of the wide visible spectrum with promoted charge separation. In addition, the possible mechanism for enhanced activity was proposed and also the active species responsible for the degradation of dyes were investigated. So this report may expect to provide an effective strategy for the design and fabrication of hetero-junctional organic–inorganic hybrid nanocomposite for photocatalytic pollutant removal.

For the preparation of g-C3N4, melamine was directly polymerized at 500 °C for 6 hrs. The g-C3N4/AgCl was synthesized by dispersing g-C3N4 powders into 100 mL of water and subjected it to ultrasonication for 30 min. Followed by a stoichiometric amount of silver nitrate was added to the solution and it was vigorously stirred to obtain a homogeneous solution. Along with the prepared solution sodium chloride was added in a dropwise manner. Finally, the mixture was refluxed at 90 °C for 2 h. The obtained greenish yellow suspension was washed and dried at 80 °C for 12 h.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from bio-waste (Citrus X sinensis) by hydrothermal method. Locally collected Citrus X sinensis peels (orange peels) were initially washed with water and dried in sunlight then it was carbonized in a hot air oven at 150 °C for 12 h. The pre-treated Citrus X sinensis peels were washed in dilute sulphuric acid followed by water then it was filtered and dried at 180 °C for 2 h. To oxidize the obtained orange peel, it was soaked in sodium hypochlorite solution for 5 h and then washed with double distilled water till it reaches a pH of 7. The resultant was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 2 h. The Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave was then cooled down naturally, and the obtained brown solution was washed with dichloromethane finally the obtained samples by centrifugation were vacuum dried.

To prepare ternary composite, a stoichiometric amount of g-C3N4 and CDs were dispersed in 150 mL water by ultra-sonication for 15 min. Then 0.118 g of silver nitrate was added in the suspension and stirred for 2 h. Then an aqueous solution of NaCl (0.05 g in 20 mL of water) was dropwise added and further stirred for 2 h. The obtained suspension was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and water then the resultant was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

Photocatalytic activity of the samples was carried out on MB and RhB dye degradation in a quartz reactor with 250 mL capacity. Initially, in the 50 mL of MB (20 mg/L) and Rh B (10 mg/L) as prepared dye solvent the as-synthesized photocatalyst (10 mg) was added. To obtain homogeneous dispersion, the solution was maintained at constant stirring in magnetic stirrer under a reaction temperature of 27–30 °C using a water circulation arrangement. Before irradiation, the suspension with dye and photocatalyst were continuously stirred in the absence of radiation for 30 min to attain adsorption and desorption equilibrium. The commercial 40 W LED bulb was used as a source of visible light because it provides high-intensity emission in the visible range. The measured average LED light intensity was approximately 50,000 lx. At regular intervals, samples were taken out and examined using UV- Spectroscopy at 664 nm for MB, 554 nm for Rh B corresponding to the maximum absorption wavelength of dyes.

To estimate the role of reactive spices in the degradation process of dye the trapping experiment was carried out by different scavengers along with catalyst under the LED light. The 0.1 mmol of Benzoquinone, 0.1 mmol of Ethylenediamine, 0.1 mmol of tetraacetic acid, and 5 mL of isopropanol were used as the superoxide anion radicle, hole (h+) and hydroxyl (
OH) radical scavengers.

To confirm the feasibility of our strategy, as-synthesized samples were initially analyzed by FESEM and TEM. Fig. 1 a shows that g-C3N4 possess irregular morphology with a flake like structure with non-uniform, porous folding structures. The irregular morphology of g-C3N4 can be assigned to the facile agglomeration of layered thin nanosheets. The wrinkled structure of pure g-C3N4 provides great support to attach carbon dots and AgCl particles. Fig. 1b shows that the formation of CD/g-C3N4/AgCl composites was highly packed and agglomerated nanostructures compared to that of pure g-C3N4 was obtained. The presence of composites was hardly observed in FESEM images which might be due to the stacking of g-C3N4 sheets. Fig. 1c depicts a TEM image of C-dots revealing the homogeneous dispersion of particles with a spherical morphology in narrow size distribution (Fig. 1d) of 2–9 nm in diameter. The SAED pattern (Fig. 1c (inset)) portrays the amorphous nature of CDs i.e. since the crystallization is the week and the lattice stripes were unseen. On closer observation with TEM, the micrograph of ternary composite (Fig. 1e) confirms the anchoring of AgCl and CDs onto the surface of g-C3N4 forming an interface. Also, the interplanar spacing of 0.34 and 0.28 obtained from HRTEM corresponds to the plane of g-C3N4 and AgCl respectively.

The XRD analysis was utilized to investigate the crystalline structure, and phase purity of g-C3N4 synthesized via poly-condensation and its corresponding variation with the addition of AgCl and AgCl/CD composites. From the XRD pattern in Fig. 2 (a), it was observed that the pure g-C3N4 possess two characteristic peaks at 27.73(002) and 13.1(100) corresponds to the long-range interplanar stacking of aromatic system and in-plane structural packing, i.e., the consecutive distance between the holes of tris triazine pore6. The additional peaks observed at g-C3N4/AgCl XRD pattern indicates the inclusion of AgCl, i.e. the presence of new peaks at 2θ = (200), (220), (311) planes corresponding to AgCl nanoparticles. Obviously after the incorporation of CDs on the surface of g-C3N4/AgCl the obtain XRD pattern of the nanocomposite was analogous to g-C3N4/AgCl composite and shows decreases intensity with no other peaks indicating the low content of CD in the composite. Also, compared with binary, the diffraction peak of g-C3N4 in the ternary composite at 27.73° shifted slightly to 27.79° which was ascribed to successful intercalation of CDs on the host matrix. The peaks corresponding to the possible impurities were not observed implying the purity of the prepared samples.

The FT-IR spectrum of pure and composites was compared in Fig. 2(b) to determine the existing functional groups. Characteristic vibration modes to its corresponding samples were obtained. In the g-C3N4 and its composites spectra, the major absorption band in the range of 805,1240–1600 and 3100–3300 cm−1 are attributed to the breathing mode of s-triazine units, stretching vibration of CN and CN in heterocycle. Compared with g-C3N4 carbon dot exhibits different absorption peak centered at 788,1625,1710,286,2941 and 3430 cm−1 which was assigned to the CO, CH3, CC, CO, CH2, CH3 and OH functional group respectively. In comparison with g-C3N4, a similar spectrum of its composites were obtained, which might be due to (i) the superimposed band position for intense hepetazine ring in g-C3N4 with CC in carbon (ii) the low content of CDs and (iii) usually AgCl based photocatalyst does not possess any absorption peak for AgCl bond.

The optical property of the prepared samples was investigated by UV diffused reflectance spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 2c the absorption band edge of g-C3N4 is about 460 nm which is due to π-π* or n-π* electron transition in the ring whereas for g-C3N4 /AgCl composite it is approximate of 440 nm. The observed blue shift for g-C3N4 /AgCl band edge is assigned the addition of AgCl which possess large band gap with absorption band edge nearly at 380 nm. In the case of the ternary composite, the absorption band edge is slightly higher than g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/AgCl composite. Fig. 2 (c-inset) shows the graphical evaluation of the bandgap of the as-synthesized products. It is seen that g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/AgCl nanocomposites possess absorption band edge near 460 and 440 nm has a band gap of 2.7 and 2.81 eV respectively. Then it is evident that the band gap of g-C3N4/AgCl/CD lies in between pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/AgCl due to the formation of heterojunction with CDs. Hence under irradiation of the visible-light, more electron-hole pairs are produced in the ternary nanocomposite, leads to efficient photocatalytic activity relative to pure and g-C3N4/AgCl composite. The efficient tool to investigate charge carrier recombination effects of photocatalysts were carried out by Photoluminescence analysis. Fig. 2(d) shows the photoluminescence spectra of the prepared samples. Addition of AgCl and CD with g-C3N4 exhibit lowest emission intensity implying the active interaction among g-C3N4, AgCl, and CD. As a result of a maximum reduction in recombination rate of e- and h+ pair it migrates to the surface and facilitates enhanced photocatalytic activity.

XPS was employed to study the detailed state of elements and chemical environment of ternary (g-C3N4/AgCl/CD) nanocomposite. In Fig. 3 (a) the wide XPS survey spectrum is composed of C 1s, N 1s, Ag 3d, Cl 2p, and O 1s elements suggesting the purity of prepared samples. The high-resolution spectrum of the C 1s region in the g-C3N4/AgCl/CD shows peaks at the binding energies of 284.75 eV and 288.38 eV corresponding to the sp2 (cc) and sp2 hybridized carbon (NCN) a backbone of g-C3N4 [24–26]. Further, the peak positioned at 293.73 eV belongs to the CO group representing the CDs particles (Fig. 3(b)). The two peaks centered at 398.68 eV and 400.06 eV in Fig. 3(c) are ascribed to the sp2 hybridized aromatic nitrogen in the triazine unit and N-(C)3 functional groups in the g-C3N4 [27,28] . The characteristic peaks of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 were observed at the binding energies of 367.68 eV and 373.76 eV respectively represents its oxidation state (Ag+) in AgCl (Fig. 3(d)) [29]. The Cl 2p spectrum in Fig. 3(e) was deconvoluted into Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 at the binding energies of 198.10 eV and 199.67 eV and are attributed to Cl- in the ternary product [30]. In Fig. 3(f) the sole peak with two components at binding energies of 531.93 eV and 532.58 eV corresponds to the chemical environment of COH/COC and CO groups respectively [31].

The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples was studied by the degradation of MB and RhB dye under LED light illumination at ambient condition. Fig. 4 (a, b) indicates the plot between time and concentration of dye at various time (t). It is evident from Fig. 4(a, b) the degradation of MB and Rh B dyes without catalysts shows negligible efficiency by photolysis process. The degradation efficiency of organic pollutants increased in the order of Pure < binary composite < ternary composite. The poor performance of pure g-C3N4 was ascribed to high recombination rate of charge carriers and its inability to harvest visible light. Fig. 4(c) indicates that the MB degradation efficiency of the ternary composite is higher than those pure and binary composite. To understand the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of the ternary composite, the proposed possible electron-hole transport behaviour was illustrated in schematic diagram 1. It is necessary to calculate the valence and conduction band energy for effective charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic behaviour of the ternary nanocomposite. The conduction band and valance band energy of the g-C3N4 and AgCl was calculated applying the below formula
(1)
E
C B
= χ -
E e
- 0.5
E g
(2)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+ E g
Here, ECB and EVB describe the energy level of conduction band and valance band respectively, χ denotes the electronegativity, Ee represent the energy of free electron in hydrogen scale, Eg denote the bandgap energy.

The calculated energy of the g-C3N4 was −1.13 eV (ECB), 1.57 eV (EVB) and for AgCl to be −0.06 eV (ECB), 3.19 eV (EVB). When the photons are incident on the surface of g-C3N4 the valance band electrons are excited to the conduction band. The accumulated photo induced electrons in the conduction band of g-C3N4 are transferred to the conduction band of AgCl (i.e the electrons are transferred from high negative energy to less negative energy) through the CD which acts as an electron acceptor. The holes are remains in the valance band of g-C3N4 [4].

The electron at the conduction band of the AgCl has more potential than standard potential to produce the hydroxyl radicals (0.68 eV). Whereas the electrons from the CB of the g-C3N4 react with atmospheric oxygen and it produces the superoxide radicals. On the other hand the holes at the VB of g-C3N4 directly oxidize the dye molecule and liberate the CO2 and H2O.

In this way the photo-induced electron hole pairs are separated and the charge carrier recombination effect was greatly reduced. This result was confirmed by the emission peak intensity reduced for ternary photocatalyst in PL spectroscopy results. Hence, the hetero junctional g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs photocatalyst would be a potential candidate for the degradation of organic dyes under visible light irradiation.

The calculated MB degradation efficiency was 98% for (g-C3N4/AgCl/CD) which is higher than the g-C3N4/AgCl (14%) and pure g-C3N4 (10%) over 75 min of LED light illumination. The rate constant for the dye degradation process was calculated by following relation
(3)
l n ( C /
C 0
) = - k t
where C0 denotes the concentration of dye solution at time t = 0 and C refers to the concentration of dye solution at time ‘t’, k refers the rate constant.

As shown in Fig. 4(c) the rate constant of MB dye degradation was studied and calculated for catalysts. The rate constant for the ternary composite was 0.04593 min−1 which are 20 times higher than binary composite (0.00235 min−1) and 38 folds higher than the pristine g-C3N4 (0.00121 min−1). Fig. 4(b, d) demonstrate the RhB dye degradation efficiency and rate constant. The calculated RhB dye degradation efficiency of the ternary composite was 90% after 75 min of light irradiation which is higher than the other prepared catalysts. The efficiency of g-C3N4 and binary were 11% and 22% respectively. The rate constant for Rh B dye degradation process was 0.023, 0.003, 0.0013 min−1 for g-C3N4/AgCl/CD, g-C3N4/AgCl, and g-C3N4 respectively. Hence form the degradation process it is concluded that the ternary composite exhibits the best performance compared to binary and pure catalysts in visible light [20]. The improved photocatalytic performance of ternary composite (Fig. 1s) was due to the presence CD in the heterojunction between g-C3N4 and AgCl which acts as an electron acceptor to promote the electron movement and efficiently suppresses the photo exciton recombination.

To further detect the main active species for the dye degradation process were investigated by elemental trapping experiment conducted on MB dye at ambient environmental condition along with scavengers. The Benzoquinone (BQ), Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), and isopropanol (IPA) were used as the superoxide, hole and hydroxyl radicals as scavengers. From the trapping experimental plot Fig. 5 (a) shows that the dye degradation process significantly suppressed by addition of BQ which reduce the degradation efficiency of ternary composites. The superoxide radical scavenger reduces efficiency by up to 29%. The addition of EDTA and IPA could not change any degradation reaction efficiency. Hence the scavengers trapping experiment concluded that the O2 – radicals rate the main active spices for the degradation process.

The stability of the prepared photocatalyst was studied by recycling the catalyst for dye degradation which is shown in Fig. 5(b). After fourth cycle the degradation ability of the composite catalyst was reduced to 93% on MB degradation. The reduced efficiency may be caused by the solubility nature of the AgCl nanoparticles. Also, few Ag cations in the composite would catch electrons from the conduction band of silver chloride and may raise a trace amount of metallic Ag along the composite which is a common phenomenon occurring in Ag based photocatalysts [11,32]. Even though the developed Ag nanoparticles can enhance the transmitting of generated e- within the nanocomposite, because of reduction response the stableness of the ternary composite would be reduced. In spite, g-C3N4/AgCl/CD catalyst has reasonable stability. Therefore, it can be employed for organic pollutant removal for industrial application. Furthermore, from the obtained Powder XRD pattern (Fig. 5(c)) before and after degradation, significant changes in structure were not observed; hence the composite is stable to a fair extent.

To understand the improved photocatalytic reaction mechanism of ternary nanocomposite for dye degradation process was illustrated in Scheme 1 . When the photons are incident on the surface of g-C3N4 the valance band electrons are excited to the conduction band. The accumulated photoinduced electrons in the conduction band of g-C3N4 are transferred to the conduction band of AgCl (Electrons are transferred from high negative energy to less negative energy) through the CD which acts as an electron acceptor. The holes are remains in the valance band of g-C3N4. The photo-excited electrons in AgCl reduce the water into H2O2 and which is further react with a dye molecule to produce CO2 and water. On the other hand, the electrons in the conduction band of g-C3N4 are reacted with atmospheric oxygen to produces the superoxide radicals which degrade the dye molecules. The holes in the valance band of g-C3N4 directly oxidized the dye molecule into water and carbon dioxide. Hence a hetero junctional g-C3N4/AgCl/CDs photocatalyst would be a potential candidate for the degradation of organic dyes under visible light irradiation.

In summary, the simple approach was adopted for the synthesis of the eco-friendly ternary heterojunction. The as-prepared ternary composite exhibits enhanced photocatalytic performance in the degradation of organic dyes under visible light irradiation. The ternary nanocomposite illustrated a high photocatalytic activity which was nearly 10 and 51 fold higher than pure g-C3N4 in the degradation of MB and RhB dye. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was due to the synergistic effect of g-C3N4, AgCl, and CDs which actively suppress the rate of recombination of charge carriers and its ability of broad visible light absorption. Despite its enhanced catalytic activity, it shows considerable stability over degradation cycles which would be a promising candidate for dye degradation. Therefore heterogeneous hybrids could open a new avenue for efficient visible light driven photocatalyst for environmental remediation.
One of the authors Priyadharshini Matheswaran acknowledges Periyar University for providing financial support in the form of University Research Fellowship(URF) grant.
